summary: This notebook delivers a comprehensive exploratory data analysis and feature derivation pipeline for a wearable sensor dataset designed to detect Body-Focused Repetitive Behaviors (BFRBs). It systematically examines data quality, focusing on missing value patterns across IMU, thermopile, and time-of-flight sensors, and visualizes univariate and multivariate distributions of both sensor readings and demographic metadata. The author computes sequence-level aggregations (mean, std, max) from raw time-series data and derives physical features like acceleration magnitude and wrist rotation angle. Key takeaways include the necessity of handling variable-length sequences, managing multimodal sensor dropout in the test set, and addressing moderate class imbalance across 18 gesture classes.
competition_flow: Loads raw train/test CSVs and demographics, performs extensive missingness and distribution analysis, derives sequence-level aggregated features from raw time-series sensor data, and visualizes relationships to inform future modeling.
data_reading: pd.read_csv() loads train.csv, test.csv, train_demographics.csv, and test_demographics.csv from Kaggle input paths.
data_processing: Treats -1 values in Time-of-Flight sensors as missing (replace(-1, np.nan)).
Groups time-series data by sequence_id to compute sequence-level aggregations.
No explicit normalization, scaling, or augmentation is applied.
features_engineering: Computes acceleration magnitude (acc_mag) and wrist rotation angle (rot_angle) from raw IMU data.
Derives sequence-level aggregations (mean, std, max) for acc_mag and rot_angle.
Calculates per-timestamp mean across 64 ToF pixels per sensor, then aggregates mean/std per sequence.
Computes mean of 5 thermopile sensors per timestamp, then aggregates mean/std per sequence.
insights: Gestures vary widely in duration (up to 700 steps), requiring models to handle variable-length inputs rather than fixed windows.
Time-of-Flight sensors exhibit ~60% missingness in training, and the hidden test set will likely have ~50% of sequences with only IMU data, necessitating robust handling of multimodal sensor dropout.
The target distribution spans 18 classes with moderate imbalance (top classes ~10%, tail ~1.5-2%), indicating a need for class-balanced sampling or weighted loss.
IMU, thermopile, and ToF sensors provide complementary signals (motion dynamics, skin contact, spatial proximity) that should be combined rather than relying on IMU alone.
critical_findings: ToF missingness is structurally encoded as -1 rather than standard NaN, requiring explicit handling to avoid skewing statistics.
The public test.csv is explicitly warned against using for final model decisions, as the hidden leaderboard test is entirely different and accessed via API.
Sequence-level missingness varies significantly by sensor type, with ToF having high partial missingness and thermopile having ~5-8% partially missing sequences, while IMU is nearly complete.
